content:
Key Findings

  • Hyndburn Borough Council paid 2,395 unique suppliers between 2016/17 and 2025/26
  • 674 suppliers received under £1,000 each, representing 28% of all suppliers
  • Only 17 suppliers received over £1 million each
  • The council made 29,804 transactions totalling £211.3 million

The Long Tail: Hyndburn's Hundreds of Small Suppliers

Hyndburn Borough Council has paid 2,395 different suppliers over the past decade, with a significant portion of these transactions going to smaller suppliers. This "long tail" of suppliers—those receiving smaller amounts—raises questions about procurement efficiency and potential savings through consolidation.

The data shows that 674 suppliers received under £1,000 each, accounting for 28% of all suppliers. In contrast, only 17 suppliers received over £1 million each. This distribution suggests a fragmented procurement landscape, where the council is managing a large number of small contracts alongside a few significant ones.

Supplier Size Distribution

The majority of Hyndburn's suppliers fall into the smaller categories. Specifically, 1,008 suppliers received between £1,000 and £10,000, and 545 suppliers received between £10,000 and £100,000. This middle tier represents a substantial portion of the supplier base, indicating a diverse range of procurement needs.

On the higher end, 151 suppliers received between £100,000 and £1 million, and just 17 suppliers received over £1 million. This small group of large suppliers likely includes key contractors for significant projects or services.

Potential for Consolidation

With 2,395 unique suppliers, Hyndburn Borough Council manages a complex procurement landscape. The question arises: could consolidation of smaller suppliers into larger contracts save money and streamline processes? For example, consolidating purchases from multiple small suppliers into fewer, larger contracts could reduce administrative overhead and potentially secure better pricing through bulk purchasing.

However, consolidation must be balanced against the need for competition and diversity in procurement. A diverse supplier base can foster innovation and ensure that the council has access to a wide range of goods and services. The challenge is to find the right balance between efficiency and maintaining a competitive marketplace.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to Hyndburn Borough Council about their procurement consolidation strategies and potential savings
  • Attend council committee meetings to learn more about procurement policies and discuss potential improvements
  • Check the council's published contracts register to see which suppliers are receiving the largest contracts
  • Write to your local councillor about the potential benefits and drawbacks of consolidating smaller suppliers into larger contracts
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data and analyse supplier distributions for yourself
This article is based on Hyndburn Borough Council's published spending data covering 2016/17 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
